coredata.dc:title: A fluorescence and resonance Rayleigh scattering di-model probe was developed for trace K+ coupled N-doped carbon dot and aptamer
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xiyin
Jiang, Xin
Zhang, Zhihao
Li, Jiao
Liang, Aihui
Jiang, Zhiliang
coredata.dc:description: The nitrogen-doped carbon dot (CDUN) was rapidly prepared by microwave method, using trisodium citrate as carbon source and urea as nitrogen source, and was characterized by fluorescence, absorption, resonance Rayleigh scattering (RRS), infrared and electron microscopy. The CDUN produced a fluorescence peak at 450 nm and a RRS peak at 375 nm, with an excited wavelength of 375 nm. When the aptamer (Apt) was added, the CDUN was encapsulated by Apt and reduced its fluorescence and RRS intensities. When K+ was added, it reacts with Apt to form a stable G-quadruplex and free CDUN. The fluorescence and RRS di-model intensity was enhanced linearly with the concentration of K+. Accordingly, a simple, rapid and sensitive di-model molecular spectral method for the determination of 0.008–0.27 μmoL/L K+ has been developed, with a detection limit of 0.3 nmoL/L.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Potassium ion
Aptamer
Carbon dot
Fluorescence
RRS

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The excitation and fluorescence spectra of carbon dots
The fluorescence enhancement principle of CDN
Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analysis principle of detecting trace K+ by fluorescence/RRS dual-mode analysis method.
Excitation spectra of CDUN a: 0.73 mg/L CDUN; b: 5.86 mg/L CDUN; c: 23.4 mg/L CDUN; d: 46.9 mg/L CDUN; e: 93.8 mg/L CDUN; f: 187.5 mg/L CDUN; g: 375 mg/L CDUN.
Fluorescence spectra of CDUN a: 0 mg/L CDUN; b: 0.4 mg/L CDUN; c: 1.5 mg/L CDUN; d: 3 mg/L CDUN; e:5.9 mg/LCDUN; f: 11.7 mg/L CDUN; g: 23.4 mg/L CDUN; h: 46.9 mg/L CDUN; i: 93.8 mg/L CDUN.
Fluorescence spectra of 187.5 mg/L CDUN with different excited wavelengths.

Fluorescence spectra of Apt-BS-CDUN-K+ system a: 0.08 μmoL/L Apt +20 mg/L CDUN +10 mmoL/L BS; b: a+0.03 μmoL/L K+; c: a+0.08 μmoL/L K+; d: a+0.13 μmoL/L K+; e: a+0.2 μmoL/L K+; f: a+0.23 μmoL/L K+; g: a+0.27 μmoL/L K+.

Emission spectra of the Apt-BS-CDUN-K+ dual-mode system with λ ex of 370 nm. a: 0.08 μmoL/L Apt +20 mg/L CDUN +10 mmoL/L BS; b: a+0.03 μmoL/L K+; c: a+0.08 μmoL/L K+; d: a+0.13 μmoL/L K+; e: a+0.2 μmoL/L K+; f: a+0.23 μmoL/L K+; g: a+0.27 μmoL/L K+.
Absorption spectra of CDUN a: 0.01 g/L CDUN; b: 0.02 g/L CDUN; c: 0.05 g/L CDUN; d: 0.09 g/L CDUN; e: 0.19 g/L CDUN; f: 0.38 g/L CDUN.
RRS spectra of CDUN a: 0.75 g/L CDUN; b: 0.38 g/L CDUN; c: 0.19 g/L CDUN; d: 0.1 g/L CDUN; e: 0.09 g/L CDUN; f: 0.08 g/L CDUN; g: 0.06 g/L CDUN.
Fluorescence enhancement mechanism of nitrogen-doped carbon dots.
Infrared spectra of 8.16 mg/mL CD0 (a), 1.5 mg/mL CDUN (b), 0.23 mg/mL CDTN (c) 1.67 mg/mL CDCN (d) and 1.67 mg/mL CDAN (e).
Scanning electron microscope of CD0 (a), CDUN (b), CDTN (c), CDCN (d) and CDAN (e); transmission electron microscope of CD0 (f), CDUN (g), CDTN (h), CDCN (i) and CDAN (j).
The influence of pH, buffer, CDUN and Apt concentration on Δ F.
Di-mode working curve of the Apt- BS-CDUN-K+ system.

The stability of Carbon dots CDUN
a .

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The concentration of carbon dots CDUN was 0.38 g/L.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analytical characteristics comparison of fluorescence detection of K+.
Analytical method comparison of K+ detection by aptamer.

row.entry: A colorimetric sensor is used to detect K+.
G-quadruplex binds to the target to restore the fluorescence.
Optical detection of K+ using quantum-dot-based Apt nanosensor.
Apt biosensor for impedance spectroscopy detection of K+ based on DNA G-quadruplex conformation.
K+ was detected by guanine-rich oligonucleotide K+ fluorescence analysis platform.
K+ combined with Apt to form G-quadruplex to cause CDUN Flu and RRS enhancing.

head.ce:title: A fluorescence and resonance Rayleigh scattering di-model probe was developed for trace K+ coupled N-doped carbon dot and aptamer

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Ecology of Rare and Endangered Species and Environmental Protection (Guangxi Normal University), Ministry of Education, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Theory and Technology, Guilin, 541004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Ecology of Rare and Endangered Species and Environmental Protection (Guangxi Normal University)
Guangxi Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Theory and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Ecology of Rare and Endangered Species and Environmental Protection (Guangxi Normal University), Ministry of Education, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Theory and Technology, Guilin 541004, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Xin Jiang and Xiyin Wang contributed equally to this article.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The nitrogen-doped carbon dot (CDUN) was rapidly prepared by microwave method, using trisodium citrate as carbon source and urea as nitrogen source, and was characterized by fluorescence, absorption, resonance Rayleigh scattering (RRS), infrared and electron microscopy. The CDUN produced a fluorescence peak at 450 nm and a RRS peak at 375 nm, with an excited wavelength of 375 nm. When the aptamer (Apt) was added, the CDUN was encapsulated by Apt and reduced its fluorescence and RRS intensities. When K+ was added, it reacts with Apt to form a stable G-quadruplex and free CDUN. The fluorescence and RRS di-model intensity was enhanced linearly with the concentration of K+. Accordingly, a simple, rapid and sensitive di-model molecular spectral method for the determination of 0.008–0.27 μmoL/L K+ has been developed, with a detection limit of 0.3 nmoL/L.

Fluorescence and resonance Rayleigh scattering di-model probe for trace K+ coupled N-doped carbon dot and aptamer.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence was a convenient and sensitive molecular spectral technique [1–6]. Some new sensitive and selective fluorescent analysis methods were established, combined with the specific aptamer (Apt) [7–10]. Sun et al. established a label-free fluorescent Apt sensor in order to detect 0.05–100 μg/mL tetracycline which was based on the tetracycline-binding Apt and thiazole orange (TO). TO was non-fluorescent and when it was embedded in a tetracycline-binding Apt of a G-quadruplex structure, the fluorescence enhanced. When the target tetracycline was existent, the conformational change in the G-quadruplex structure can lead to the fluorescence quenching from TO Ref. [11]. Li et al. developed a bisphenol A (BPA) analysis method based on Apt-gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) fluorescence internal filtering effect of CdTe quantum dots, with linear range of 10–80 ng/mL and a detection limit of 1.86 ng/mL [12]. Although there were some methods based on Apt fluorescent detection, most of the fluorescent methods required a modified Apt. These procedures were time-wasting and costly, it may also cause an influence on the binding of the Apt to the target due to the characteristic of the modified radical group Apt. Thereby reduced the sensitivity of the detection. Free-labeled Apt fluorescence analysis technology was simple, rapid, and sensitive, and can be applied to food monitoring [13], clinical testing [14], chemical analysis [15] and other area. Wang et al. [16] reported that the specific cocaine-mediated ATMND displacement reaction and a fluorescence ratiometric method. Because of the high affinity of free-labeled Apts and good fluorescence quantum yield, with a linear range of 0.10–1002 μmoL/L and the detection limit of 56 nmoL/L. Qiu et al. developed an isothermal circular strand-displacement amplification (ICSDA) and graphene oxide absorption(GO) label-free Apt fluorescence sensor for 0.2–100 μmoL/L cocaine [17]. Pan et al. develop a label-free Apt fluorescence probe for the detection of 3.0–120 nmoL/L interferon gamma, with detection limit of 2.0 nmoL/L [18]. Di-mode analysis was a new method with high sensitivity and good stability. Some di-mode analysis methods were reported, such as fluorescence/colorimetric di-mode analysis [19], fluorescence/SERS di-mode analysis [20], fluorescence/ultraviolet di-mode analysis [21], fluorescence/RRS di-mode analysis [22]. Su et al. have developed a dual-mode electronic biosensor with high selectivity and stability for the detection of microRNA-21, based on gold nanoparticle-decorated MoS2 nanosheet. Such MoS2 -based biosensor exhibited excellent performance for miRNA-21 detection in the range from 10 fmoL/L - 1 nmoL/L with detection limit of 0.78 fmoL/L and 0.45 fmoL/L for differential pulse voltammetry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy technique, respectively [23]. Zhu et al. used di-mode fluorescent probe to detect 20–100 ng/mL melamine with a detection limit of 8 ng/mL, based on gold nanoparticles aggregation-induced fluorescence “turn-on” and “turn-off” of CdTe quantum dots [24]. Liu et al. proposed a SERS/fluorescence di-mode nanosensor based on the coordination chemistry principle of T-Hg2+-T. Using this method, the detection limit of fluorescence detection is 0.5 nmoL/L in the linear range of 0.5–500 nmoL/L, and that of SERS detection is 5 pmoL/L with a linear range of 5 pmoL/L-50 nmoL/L [25]. The above di-mode analytical methods need two instruments with disadvantages of high cost and complex. Resonance Rayleigh scattering (RRS) was a simple and sensitive molecular spectral technique with commonly used fluorescence meter [26]. Two hydride generation-catalytic RRS and fluorescence methods were reported for the determination of trace arsenic. However, the both techniques were not coupling well, and its determinations were independent including recording and working curve. Therefore, it was significant to develop simple and low-cost, sensitive and selective di-model fluorescence and RRS coupled-method with a fluorescence meter and carbon dot (CD) probes.

CDs was the new type of zero-dimensional carbon materials with good stability, excellent optical properties and good biocompatibility. It was also excellent in low toxicity, and water solubility. There are some methods to synthesize CDs, such as laser erosion [27], electrochemical synthesis [28], chemical oxidation [29] and hydrothermal synthesis [30]. Rong et al. used guanidine chloride and citric acid as catalysts to prepare high fluorescent nitrogen-carbon doped (N-CD) by using the method of solid phase pyrolysis. It emits strong blue fluorescence and has high stability which can be used to measure 0–200 μmoL/L Fe3+ with the detection limit of 100 nmoL/L [31]. Xiao et al. established a hollow nitrogen-doped carbon microspheres (HNCMS) as a new type of carbon material for the electrochemical determination of 5–30 moL/L uric acid (UA), 100–1000 moL/L ascorbic acid (AA) and 3–75 moL/L dopamine (DA) with respective detection limits of 0.04 moL/L, 0.91 moL/L and 0.02 moL/L [32]. Microwave synthesis is a new and efficient method for the quickly preparation of carbon dots [33]. Li et al. developed a microwave method to synthesize carbon quantum dots(CQs) with beta-cyclodextrin carbon sources and used the method of quenching CQS fluorescence with quercetin for detection with a linear range of 0.001–0.8 mmoL/L [34]. Yang et al. used ascorbic acid as a carbon source and l-lysine as a passivator to synthesize luminescent carbon quantum dots by microwave method. A new method for the determination of ranitidine hydrochloride by using carbon quantum dots as fluorescence probes was established, which was based on the quenching of carbon quantum dots by ranitidine hydrochloride with a linear range of 1.0–70 moL/L and a detection limit of 71 nmoL/L [35]. However, there has been no report on microwave preparation for CDUN that was sued to determine K+ by the non-labeled Apt fluorescence method.

K+ was a common metal ion and an important element to human body, which could affect people’ s heart structure and neuromuscular function [36]. There are some methods to detective K+, such as ion selective electrodes (ISE) [37], atomic absorption spectrometry [38], photometric method [39], resonance scattering spectroscopy [40] and fluorescence method [41]. Jarczewska et al. used the Au–S bond to adsorb the thiolated aptamer probe to the gold disk electrode to determine the concentration of potassium ion with a linear range of 10−8-10−5 moL/L, and a detection limit of 2.31 × 10−9 moL/L [42]. Chen et al. developed a colorimetric method to detect K+ with a linear range of 5 nmoL/L-1 mol/Land a detection limit of 5 nmoL/L [43]. Ma et al. developed a method to detect K+ based on aptamer and pyrene-labeled fluorescent biosensor with a linear range of 6.3 × 10−4-1.0 × 10−2 moL/L and a detection limit of 5.0 × 10−4 moL/L [44]. Cai et al. established an aptamer-modified nanosilver RRS for the detection of 0.06–3350 μmoL/L K+ in serum with a detection limit of 0.006 μmoL/L [45]. In this paper, on the basis of preparing N-doped carbon dots by microwave and combing with the aptamer reaction of K+, a quick and efficient fluorescence and RRS di-mode coupled method for the determination of trace K+ was established.

Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi High-Technologies Corporation, Tokyo, Japan); TU-1901 double beam UV-visible light spectrophotometer (Beijing General Instrument Co. LTD, Beijing, China); HH-S2 electric-heated thermostatic water bath (Jintan Land Automation Instrument Factory, Jintan, China); SYZ-550 Quartz sub-boiling distiller (Jiangsu Crystal Glass Instrument Factory, Nanjing, China); SK3300B ultrasonic cleaner (Shanghai Kedao Ultrasonic Instrument Co., LTD, Shanghai, China); and 16 K desk centrifuge (Zhuhai Black Horse Medical Instrument Co., LTD, Zhuhai, China) were used.

Urea; tripolycyanamide; cysteine (Cys); ammonium sulfite; 0.03 g/mL trisodium citrate; 0.01 mol/L KCl storage solution; 25 μmol/L Apt solution, sequence: 5′-3′ GGG TTA GGG TTA GGG TTA GGG (Shanghai Sangon Biological Engineering co., LTD.); 0.2 moL/L disodium hydrogen phosphate solution; 0.1 moL/L sodium citrate solution were used. The preparation of buffer (PBS): 1229 mL of sodium citrate solution was added into 771 mL of disodium hydrogen phosphate solution to obtain a buffer of pH 4. The concentration of the buffer solution was calculated by the sodium citrate.

Preparation of CD0 carbon dot: 1 g of trisodium citrate was dissolved by ultrasonic in 30 mL water, then transferred to a high beaker which was covered by plastic wrap with air holes. It was heated in microwave for 3 min and 10 s. After the reaction, it was diluted to 30 mL with deionized water, then the CD0 solution with a carbon dot concentration of 8.16 mg/mL by calculating the total amount of carbon was obtained.

Preparation of CDUN carbon dot: 1 g of trisodium citrate and 1 g urea were dissolved by ultrasonic in 30 mL water to form a transparent solution, then transferred to a high beaker which was covered by plastic wrap with air holes. It was heated in microwave for 6 min and a half. After the reaction, it was cooled down to the room temperature then was diluted to 30 mL with water to obtain a pale yellow solution with a carbon dot concentration of 1.5 mg/mL by calculating the total amount of carbon.

Preparation of CDTN carbon dot: 1 g trisodium citrate and 0.1 g tripolycyanamide were dissolved by ultrasonic in 30 mL water to form a transparent solution, then transferred to a high beaker which was covered by plastic wrap with air holes. It was heated in microwave for 4 min. After the reaction, it was diluted to 30 mL with deionized water, then the CDTN solution with a carbon dot concentration of 0.23 mg/mL by calculating the total amount of carbon was obtained.

Preparation of CDCN carbon dot: 1 g trisodium citrate and 0.8 g cysteine were dissolved by ultrasonic in 30 mL water to form a transparent solution, then transferred to a high beaker which was covered by plastic wrap with air holes. It was heated in microwave for 6 min. After the reaction, it was diluted to 30 mL with deionized water, then the CDCN solution with a carbon dot concentration of 1.34 mg/mL by calculating the total amount of carbon was obtained.

Preparation of CDAN carbon dot: 1 g trisodium citrate and 1.2 g ammonium sulfite were dissolved by ultrasonic in 30 mL water to form a transparent solution, then transferred to a high beaker which was covered by plastic wrap with air holes. It was heated in microwave for 8 min. After the reaction, it was diluted to 30 mL with deionized water, then the CDAN solution with a carbon dot concentration of 8.16 mg/mL by calculating the total amount of carbon was obtained.

In a 5 mL corked scale tube, 20 μL of 1.5 mg/mL CDUN solution, 50 μL of 2.5 μmoL/L Apt and 150 μL of pH 4 disodium hydrogen phosphate-sodium citrate buffer solution (BS) were mixed and diluted to 1.5 mL with water, then stood for 3 min. The fluorescence intensity F at 450 nm was measured by adopting the excitation wavelength of 370 nm ΔF = F–F0 was calculated by taking F0 as blank without K+. Similarly, the RRS intensity at 370 nm ΔIRRS = IRRS-(IRRS)0, and ΔI = ΔIRRS+ΔF were obtained.

CDUN was a small nanoparticle with some carboxyl, hydroxyl, amino and hydrophobic groups. The Apt contained guanine (G), thymine (T) and adenine (A) bases, and hydrophobic groups. In the pH 4 buffer solution, when the Apt and CDUN co-existed, both combined by means of intermolecular forces such as Coulomb, hydrogen bond, hydrophobic and aromatic ring accumulation forces, the CDUN probes were encapsulated by Apt, which leaded to the weakening of fluorescence and RRS intensity. After the addition of K+, the carbon dots and the nucleic acid aptamer generated a specific binding, formed a stable and hydrophobic G-quartet complex particles and free carbon dots. At the meanwhile, the carbon dots were released. With the increase of K+ concentration, the more carbon dots desorbed, the stronger fluorescence and RRS signal of the system gradually increased. Thus, single mode including fluorescence and RRS, and the di-mode methods were established for determination of trace K+ (Fig. 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The excitation and fluorescence spectra of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The excitation spectra of CD0, CDUN, CDTN, CDCN and CDAN were investigated. Under the condition of volt = 350 v, excited slit = emission slit = 10 nm and the fluorescence wavelength of 400, 400, 450, 400 and 400 nm, by putting carbon dot solution into the quartz cell and scanning with a fluorescence spectrophotometer, the excitation spectra were recorded (Fig. 2 , S1-S4). Results showed that the excitation peak intensity increased due to the carbon dot fluorescence probe concentration increasing.

The fluorescence spectra of CD0, CDUN, CDTN, CDCN and CDAN were investigated. When it was at the excitation wavelengths of 325, 370, 265, 350 and 350 nm respectively, the fluorescence spectra were obtained by scanning emission wavelength (Fig. 3 , S5-S8). A fluorescent peak was occurred separately at the wavelengths of 415 nm, 450 nm, 365 nm, 430 nm, and 435 nm. As the concentration of each carbon dot increased, the intensity of the fluorescence emission peak enhanced gradually. At the same time, at different excitation wavelengths, their fluorescence spectra of 6120 mg/L CD0, 187.5 mg/L CDUN, 115 mg/L CDTN, 167.5 mg/L CDCN and 2040 mg/L CDAN solution were investigated (Fig. 4 , S9-S12). The fluorescence peak appeared at 450 nm when the exited wavelength changes from 310 to 390 nm. This indicated that the fluorescence energy band held constant when the carbon dots excited by photons.

In the system of Apt-K+-BS-CD0, there was a fluorescence peak at 415 nm with λ ex of 325 nm. With the increase of K+ concentration, the more carbon dots were released by the aptamer reaction, the stronger the fluorescent signal increased (Fig. S13). In the system of Apt-K+-BS-CDUN, with λ ex of 370 nm, there was a fluorescence peak at 430 nm. With the increase of K+ concentration, the more carbon dots were formed by the aptamer, the stronger the fluorescent signal increased (Fig. 5 ). For the system of Apt-K+-BS-CDTN, with λ ex = 265 nm, there was a fluorescence peak at 365 nm. With the increase of K+ concentration, the more carbon dots were produced by the aptamer, the stronger the fluorescent signal increased (Fig. S14). for the system of Apt-K+-BS-CDCN, with λ ex of 350 nm, there was a fluorescence peak at 365 nm. With the increase of K+ concentration, the more carbon dots were released by the aptamer, the stronger the fluorescent signal increased (Fig. S15). In the system of Apt-K+- BS-CDAN, with λ ex of 350 nm, there was a fluorescence peak at 435 nm (Fig. S16). The system of Apt-K+-BS-CDUN was chosen to study the fluorescence and the RRS di-mode detection because it was most sensitive. When λ ex was at 370 nm, a RRS peak was at 375 nm and fluorescent peak was at 450 nm. With the increase of K+ concentration, the more carbon dots were formed by the aptamer, the strongest the value of fluorescent and RRS signal was recorded (Fig. 6 ).

The absorption spectra of CD0, CDUN, CDTN, CDCN and CDAN were recorded. They were an ultraviolet absorption peak was at 325 nm, 345 nm, 335 nm, 340 nm and 360 nm for CD0, CDUN, CDTN, CDCN and CDAN respectively (Fig. S17, Fig. 7 , Fig. S18, Fig. S19, Fig. S20). The peak intensity enhanced when the concentration of each carbon dot increased.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence enhancement principle of CDN
ce:section.ce:para: Trisodium citrate was used as carbon source in the experiment. Urea participated in the reaction and provided a one-step reaction of nitrogen source in order to prepared the nitrogen-doped carbon dots (Fig. 9 ). The N atom was an electron donating atom that incorporate an appropriate amount of unshared pair electrons into the carbon source. After doping the nitrogen, the electrons on the surface of carbon dots has increased. When CDN was excited by the incident light, more surface electrons were transition to the excited state, and jump to the gap state to produce stronger fluorescence. What's more, the nitrogen-doped carbon dots have high stability.

A 10 mL of 8.16 mg/mL CD0 solution, 10 mL of 1.5 mg/mL CDUN, 10 mL of 0.23 mg/mL CDTN, 10 mL of 1.67 mg/mL CDCN and 10 mL of 8.16 mg/mL CDAN were placed in the material trays separately. They were pre-frozen for 5 h in the cold trap through the vacuum freeze dryer, then was dried at 0.1Pa. After 24 h, the obtained solid samples were mixed with a certain amount of potassium bromide, then grinded evenly in the agate mortar. The powder was pressed into tablets by a tablet press. The tablets were scanned by infrared spectrometer in order to obtain the corresponding spectra, and the blank sheet of potassium bromide was used as reference. The infrared spectra show that for CD0 (Fig. 10 a), a relatively strong infrared spectral peak respectively appeared at 1397 cm−1, 1279 cm−1, 1078 cm−1 (C–O stretching vibration); 3435 cm−1 (O–H stretching vibration); 3435 cm−1 (CC–H stretching vibration); 1586 cm−1 (COO- anti-symmetrical stretching vibration); 674 cm−1 (O–H out-of-plane bending); 840 cm−1 (C–C stretching vibration). For CDUN (Fig. 10b), a relatively strong infrared spectral peak respectively appeared at 3436 cm−1, 1672 cm−1, 1165 cm−1 (CO stretching vibration); 3436 cm−1, 2344 cm−1 (N–H stretching vibration), 1616 cm−1, 1304 cm−1 (N–H deformation vibration); 2254 cm−1, 2171 cm−1, 2254 cm−1 (CC stretching vibration of conjugate structure); 1452 (CH2 scissor vibration); 1398 cm−1 (COO− symmetrical stretching vibration); 588 cm−1 (CC–H flexural vibration); 839 cm−1, 783 cm−1 (C–H out-of-plane swing); 412 cm−1 (C–CN flexural vibration), which proved the success of doping carbon dots with N. For CDTN (Fig. 10c), a relatively strong infrared spectral peak respectively appeared at 3283 cm−1, 1680 cm−1 (CO stretching vibration); 839 cm−1, 920 cm−1, 750 cm−1 (C–H out-of-plane bending); 1201 cm−1 (C–H in-plane bending); 2970 cm−1, 2918 cm−1 (C–H anti-symmetrical stretching vibration); 3503 cm−1, 3419 cm−1, 2730 cm−1, 2549 cm−1 (N–H stretching vibration); 2216 cm−1, 1610 cm−1, 1475 cm−1, (CC stretching vibration of conjugate structure); 1408 cm−1, 1260 cm−1, 1136 cm−1, 1078 cm−1, 1064 cm−1 (C–O stretching vibration); 599 cm−1 (CC–H flexural vibration); 571 cm−1 (C–O–C deformation vibration); 547 cm−1 (N–CO flexural vibration); 547 cm−1 (C–N–C flexural vibration), which proved the success of doping carbon dots with N. For CDCN (Fig. 10d), a relatively strong infrared spectral peak respectively appeared at 3434 cm−1,1910 cm−1, 1718 cm−1 (CO stretching vibration); 2344 cm-1, 2311 cm−1 (N–H stretching vibration); 1616 cm−1 (CC stretching vibration of conjugate structure); 1406 cm−1 (CO2- symmetrical stretching vibration); 1281 cm−1 (CH2 out-of-plane bending); 660 cm−1 (CC–H flexural vibration), which proved the success of doping carbon dots with N. For CDAN (Fig. 10e), a relatively strong infrared spectrum peak respectively appeared at 3433 cm−1 (O–H stretching vibration); 2344 cm−1 (N–H stretching vibration); 1614 cm−1 (CC stretching vibration of conjugate structure) 1400 cm−1, 1145 cm−1 (C–O stretching vibration); 623 cm−1 (CC–H flexural vibration), which proved the success of doping carbon dots with N. The results show that the structures of four carbon dots are similar, and the carbon dots were successfully doped with N.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM)
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared carbon dots were diluted by a certain multiple, then the solution of 2.5 μL was dripped to the silicon slice. After natural drying of the liquid, the samples were scanned and recorded by SEM. According to SEM (Fig. 11 ), CD0, CDUN, CDTN, CDCN and CDAN tended to be spherical with an average particle size of 20 nm, 40 nm, 50 nm, 40 nm and 20 nm respectively. According to the energy spectra (ES), CD0 mainly contained C, O, Na three elements, of which the energy spectral peak of C appeared at 0.27 keV, and the atomic percentage was 32.5%; the peak of O appeared at 0.53 keV, and the atomic percentage was 44.86%; the peak of Na appeared at 0.53 keV, and the atomic percentage was 22.64%. The doping of N in CDUN was clearly seen from the energy spectrum analysis (Fig. 11b). The energy spectrum peak of N appeared at 0.39 keV, and the atomic percentage was 30.5%, which meant the carbon was successfully doped with N. The doping of N and S elements in CDTN was clearly seen from ES (Fig. 11c). The energy spectrum peak of N appeared at 0.39 keV, and the atomic percentage was 12%; The energy spectrum peak of S appeared at 2.3 keV, and the atomic percentage was 11.24%, which indicated that the carbon dot was successfully doped with N and S. The doping of N and S in CDCN was clearly seen from ES (Fig. 11d). The energy spectrum peak of N appeared at 0.39 keV, and the atomic percentage was 1.42%; The energy spectrum peak of S appeared at 2.3 keV, and the atom percentage was 7.35%, which indicated that the carbon dot was successfully doped with N and S. The doping of N in CDAN was clearly seen from ES (Fig. 11e). The energy spectrum peak of N appeared at 0.39 keV, and the atomic percentage was 17.89%. According to TEM (Fig. 11f–j), the CD0, CDUN, CDTN, CDCN and CDAN were spherical particles with an average particle size of 15 nm, 30 nm, 35 nm, 30 nm and 20 nm respectively.

The interference of coexisting ions on the fluorescence measurement of 0.1 μmoL/L K+ with Apt-BS-CDUN-K+ was detected according to the experimental method. Table 4 showed that 30 μmoL/L Zn2+, Ca2+, Ba2+，Na+, HCO3 −, CO3 2−, Cu2+, IO3 −, SO4 2−, Al3+, Br−, NH4 +, Mn2+, Fe2+, Cr3+, glutamic acid, tyrosine(Tyr), glycine(Gly) did not interfere with the determination. 15 μmoL/L I−, NO2 −, Mg2+, Hg+, l-phenylalanine，l-aspartic acid and HSA did not interfere with the determination. A 3 μmoL/L BSA did not interfere with the determination. This indicated that the label-free Apt and CDUN probes have good selectivity.

Three kinds of common potable water and 6 of serum were selected as samples and stored at room temperature. For the determination of water samples, 20 μL water samples were added into the 0.08 μmoL/L Apt +20 mg/L CDUN +10 mmoL/L BS system, then samples were processed in recovery experiments. For the determination of serum samples, 6 serum samples were diluted by 1000 times. A 1000 μL of serum samples were added into the 0.08 μmoL/L Apt +20 mg/L CDUN +10 mmoL/L BS system according to the experimental method, then the samples were processed in recovery experiments. The results are shown in Table 5 with a recovery between 90%–108% and a relative standard deviation of 1%–9.7%.

In this article, four nitrogen-doped carbon dots with high stability were synthesized by microwave oven, and the CDUN was best sensitive and was chosen for use. The decrease of fluorescence intensity occurred as the aptamer wrapped the carbon dots in the BS with a pH of 4. When a range of concentration of potassium ions was added, potassium ions combined with aptamer to form a stable structure to release carbon dots, and the fluorescence was enhanced. Based on this, a new method for fluorescent detection of potassium ions was established. The method has the advantages of simple operation, high sensitivity and good selectivity. In addition, a more sensitive di-mode fluorescence and RRS method was developed.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21667006, 21767004).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2019.116559.
